payload.tactic: while (={i} /\ c{1} = c{2} /\ n{1} = n0 /\ n{2} = n0 /\ size p{1} = size p{2} /\ 1 <= i{1} /\ size c{1} + size p{1} <= max_cipher_size /\ (p{1} <> [] => size c{1} = block_size * (i{1} - 1)) /\ (forall (cc : C.counter), (n0, cc) \in ROF.m{1} => C.toint cc < i{1}) /\ (forall (nn : nonce) (cc : C.counter), (nn, cc) \in ROF.m{1} => nn \in n0 :: BNR.lenc{1}) /\ mr0 = ROin.m{1} /\ ms0 = ROout.m{1}).

last_result.effect: EasyCrypt accepted a proof-state change; the refreshed view is based on the new committed state.
last_result.proof_state: The committed EasyCrypt proof state changed.
last_result.tactic: while (={i} /\ c{1} = c{2} /\ n{1} = n0 /\ n{2} = n0 /\ size p{1} = size p{2} /\ 1 <= i{1} /\ size c{1} + size p{1} <= max_cipher_size /\ (p{1} <> [] => size c{1} = block_size * (i{1} - 1)) /\ (forall (cc : C.counter), (n0, cc) \in ROF.m{1} => C.toint cc < i{1}) /\ (forall (nn : nonce) (cc : C.counter), (nn, cc) \in ROF.m{1} => nn \in n0 :: BNR.lenc{1}) /\ mr0 = ROin.m{1} /\ ms0 = ROout.m{1}).

source.label: Parsed from EasyCrypt pretty goal text

moves.title: Program-shape context
moves.tactic_shape: Inspect current procedure region summary: shared update c; shared update p; shared update i.
moves.applicability: Use this as route-selection context; it is not a tactic to run as-is.

moves.tactic_shape: Inspect proof obligation stack: sample_frontier / statement 1.
moves.applicability: Use this as route-selection context; it is not a tactic to run as-is.

moves.tactic_shape: Inspect one-sided sampling residual: z{2} <$ dblock; quantified witness cc, nn.
moves.applicability: Use this as route-selection context; it is not a tactic to run as-is.
Use this as route-selection context; it is not a tactic to run as-is.

moves.tactic_shape: Inspect one-sided call-site map: CCRO.cc on side 1 at statement 1.
moves.applicability: Use this as route-selection context; it is not a tactic to run as-is.

moves.tactic_shape: wp. / seq <suffix> according to the suffix shape
moves.applicability: This tactic shape still has placeholders; replace them with concrete EasyCrypt arguments from the current goal before probing or committing it.
moves.runnable_status: Not yet; replace every placeholder with concrete EasyCrypt terms from the current goal before probing or committing this tactic.
missing_input: Concrete EasyCrypt arguments for every placeholder in the displayed tactic.
how_to_complete: Use the current goal variables, hypotheses, precondition, postcondition, and visible program statements to fill the placeholders.

inspect_if_unsure.why: Shows parsed goal structure and resolved identifiers.

inspect_if_unsure.name: wp
inspect_if_unsure.why: Shows valid argument forms when this tactic has several shapes.

limitations: The displayed tactic is a template; fill every placeholder with current-goal arguments before probing or committing it.

moves.applicability: Use this as route-selection context; it is not a tactic to run as-is.
Use this as route-selection context; it is not a tactic to run as-is.

frontier_blockers.left: 3 setup statement(s): c <- c ++ take (size p) (bytes_of_block (extend p +^ z)); p <- drop block_size p; i <- i + 1
frontier_blockers.right: no right-side residual statement after this call frontier
frontier_blockers.effect: Residual statements remain after the live call frontier; their effects are not part of the callee obligation.

rows.left: 3 setup statement(s): c <- c ++ take (size p) (bytes_of_block (extend p +^ z)); p <- drop block_size p; i <- i + 1
rows.right: no right-side residual statement after this call frontier
rows.proof_read: The left side has residual statements after the live call frontier; their effects are not part of the callee obligation.

residual_frontier.effect: These statements remain after the live call frontier and are checked after the call returns.
limitations: The surface reports suffix structure only; it does not choose whether to use a call, inline, wp, or another local tactic.

call_sites.statement: z <@ CCRO( SplitD.RO_DOM( SplitC1.RO_Pair( SplitC2. RO_Pair( SplitC2.I1.RO, SplitC2.I2.RO), SplitC1.I2.RO), SplitD.ROF.RO)).cc(k, n, C.ofintd i)

frontier_alignment.summary: Frontier alignment: setup statements appear before the current frontier; sampling steps are visible inside the frontier; call sites are visible at the frontier; residual statements remain after a call frontier. Use this as a proof-reader map before choosing while/rnd/seq/call tactics.

first_instruction_alignment.left_first: no left-side setup before this frontier
first_instruction_alignment.proof_read: The visible frontier is one-sided; use a one-sided tactic, alignment step, or seq cut before applying a symmetric tactic.

rows.left: no left-side setup before this frontier
rows.right: 3 setup statement(s): c <- c ++ take (size p) (bytes_of_block z); p <- drop block_size p; i <- i + 1
rows.proof_read: The right side has setup before the shared frontier; relate it to the precondition or carry the initialized state in the next invariant/cut.

rows.left: z <@ CCRO( SplitD.RO_DOM( SplitC1.RO_Pair( SplitC2. RO_Pair( SplitC2.I1.RO, SplitC2.I2.RO), SplitC1.I2.RO), SplitD.ROF.RO)).cc(k, n, C.ofintd i)
rows.right: no matching right-side call at this frontier
rows.proof_read: This is a call frontier; compare the procedures/oracle wrappers before choosing a named call or invariant call.

rows.left: 3 setup statement(s): c <- c ++ take (size p) (bytes_of_block (extend p +^ z)); p <- drop block_size p; i <- i + 1
rows.right: no right-side residual statement after this call frontier
rows.proof_read: The left side has residual statements after the live call frontier; their effects are not part of the callee obligation.

rows.left: no matching left-side sample at this frontier
rows.proof_read: This is a sampling frontier; choose a coupling or distribution argument that connects the sampled values.

procedure_navigation.how_to_use: Positions for choosing wp/sp depth, which `if` to rcond, and where the next loop/call frontier is. Facts only — pick the tactic and the branch direction yourself.

left.statement: z <@ CCRO( SplitD.RO_DOM( SplitC1.RO_Pair( SplitC2. RO_Pair( SplitC2.I1.RO, SplitC2.I2.RO), SplitC1.I2.RO), SplitD.ROF.RO)).cc(k, n, C.ofintd i)

application_context.how_to_use: Use this section when constructing the concrete invariant inside `call (_: ...)`. This section is not a tactic. It is a checklist for constructing the concrete invariant before using `probe_tactic` or `commit_tactic`.

inspect_if_unsure.why: After you draft a concrete invariant, this preview shows the obligations it creates and can reveal missing facts or extra conjuncts that make the proof harder.

facts_and_diagnostics.fact_note: Only current-frontier facts are shown here; the full proof context artifact keeps the compiler/internal details.

notes.message: proof-state analysis layer is call_site with live procedure calls, but no callable lemma handle is currently attached. Additional bridge/call lemma context may be relevant before changing this frontier by global inlining.

One-sided sampling residual: z{2} <$ dblock; quantified witness cc, nn. The postcondition still mentions the sampled value or a quantified witness, so this may be a true losslessness side condition or leftover coupling debt.

inspect_lookup_handles.effect: All handles here are read-only manager requests; the committed EasyCrypt proof state stays unchanged.
inspect_lookup_handles.manager_note: Some requests may ask EasyCrypt or name resolution; wait for the manager result before choosing the next proof intent.

ask_manager_for.use_when: Need parsed goal shape, resolved names, or KB pattern hints.
The visible frontier contains call sites or named equiv handles may apply.
ask_manager_for.returns: call-ready/oracle-equiv context for the current frontier, plus the BODY of the procedure(s) called here (functor aliases resolved) so you can see which oracles/state your call invariant must preserve

ask_manager_for.use_when: You are at an abstract-adversary `call (_: <inv>)` and want the mechanical glob frame of the invariant before adding your own semantic conjuncts.
ask_manager_for.returns: the mechanical `={glob ...}` frame of the call invariant (shared abstract oracle modules, daemon-confirmed to apply and spawn the obligations). When a named coupling predicate fits the in-scope state it ALSO surfaces a field-threaded carrier (`inv … s1{1} s2{2}`), plus candidate inductive side-conditions (`dom ⊆ used-set`) for any random oracle the call queries. All revisable starting points — confirm the coupling and add/adjust the semantic conjuncts (key/state correspondences, set-membership).

ask_manager_for.use_when: You are considering `call (_: Inv)` and already have a concrete invariant expression you want to test first.
ask_manager_for.returns: a read-only preview of the obligations caused by that candidate invariant, including pre/init, call-preservation, oracle-side, postcondition, missing-fact signals, and extra conjuncts that may create avoidable obligations

ask_manager_for.use_when: A tactic has multiple EasyCrypt argument forms.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid tactic forms and common traps

ask_manager_for.use_when: The frontier may need indexed `sp i j` before branch or call tactics.
Mid-proof pRHL suffix surgery may need indexed `wp`.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid indexed `wp` forms and suffix-alignment traps

ask_manager_for.use_when: Statement order may need a small `swap` before `sp`, `wp`, or `sim`.
A guarded branch may need `rcondt` after a case split or invariant fact.
A guarded branch may need `rcondf` after a case split or invariant fact.
A suffix proof may need `conseq` to weaken to a smaller relation before `sim`.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid `conseq` forms and pRHL memory-annotation traps

ask_manager_for.use_when: One side may have an extra sample or need an explicit sample coupling.
A known statement-order mismatch may need an eager/lazy transformation.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid `eager` forms and when to prefer smaller surgery

ask_manager_for.use_when: LHS/RHS statement order may need swap/alignment context.
ask_manager_for.returns: swap/alignment context for the visible pRHL frontier
